title: Needle felted bird ornament
subtitle: A small hanging bird in British robin colours, sized to sit on a branch or hang from a thread
excerpt: Needle felt a small roundish bird about 7 cm long in robin colours: a red-orange breast, brown-grey back, and pale belly. A loop of thread at the top allows it to hang from a branch or Christmas tree. The bird uses the same compact oval body as other sculptural pieces, with added surface-colour detailing.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted bird ornaments are a well-established beginner project in UK craft instruction. Method draws on standard compact-form technique with surface-colour addition.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Work over a foam pad and keep fingers clear.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: The bird body is an oval of brown-grey merino. The breast

in red-orange is added to the lower front of the formed core. The tail is a short triangle of darker brown; the beak is a tiny pinch of dark roving. A loop of strong thread is stitched through the back before the piece is finished.

items.name: Dark brown merino (tail and eye ring)

content.text: Roll 8 g of brown-grey roving into a compact teardrop roughly 6 x 4 cm, wider at the breast end and narrowing slightly toward the tail. Needle-felt firmly all over with the size-38 needle until the form is dense and holds its shape on the foam mat without being touched.

Pull the red-orange roving into a very thin wisp and lay it across the lower front of the body, covering about one-third of the front surface from the belly upward. Work it in with the size-40 needle in smooth strokes following the body contour. The upper boundary of the breast colour should blend softly into the brown-grey rather than ending in a hard line: thin the wisp toward the top of the breast and felt it in lightly.

Roll 1.5 g of dark brown roving into a short triangular flap about 3 x 2 cm. Needle-felt it as a flat paddle to a firm but thin shape. Attach it to the rear of the body at a slight downward angle by stabbing through the join from multiple sides.

Roll a very small pinch of black roving to a tiny point for the beak and attach it at the front of the head with the size-40 needle. Add two tiny dark spots for the eyes, positioned symmetrically on either side of the head. A very thin circle of dark roving around each eye gives the robin its characteristic expression.

Thread a large-eyed needle with strong thread. Pass the needle through the topmost point of the bird body and back out 1 cm away, then tie the thread ends in a secure knot to form a loop. Pull the knot tight inside the felt so it is hidden. The loop should be about 8-10 cm long when folded double.

items.fix: Thin the wisp right down at the top boundary. Stab along the top edge very lightly to preserve the gradual blend.
items.cause: Too much roving used at the top of the breast, or felted too firmly at the edge
items.problem: The breast colour has a hard, obvious boundary

items.fix: Start the beak with a slightly larger pinch of roving (0.5 g) and needle-felt it at the base deep into the face core before refining to a smaller point.
items.cause: Not enough felt depth at the small attachment point
items.problem: The beak falls off during handling
